text: HE et al.: AUTOMATICALLY IDENTIFYING CVE AFFECTED VERSIONS WITH PATCHES AND DEVELOPER LOGS 917
A CCURACY (I. E ., T RUE P OSITIVE , F ALSE P OSITIVE AND F ALSE N EGATIVE ) OF R E D E B UG , VUDDY AND O UR M ETHOD
report the detailed results of 50 (out of 69 in total) vulnerabilities, including the version number of the source code, CVE-ID, vulnerability type, patch type, error type, etc.
Reliability of the NVD. Quality issues in vulnerability

databases, such as the NVD, have been noted and studied before. It is shown that the CVSS metric [11] , [44] is more suitable for enterprise software products, rather than personal software products. Nguyen et al. [18] conducted an empirical experiment on the reliability of the vulnerable Chrome versions in the NVD. They use the Chrome vulnerabilities with the bug-ﬁxes IDs to manually verify the inaccuracy of the NVD report, but they cannot handle the situation without the bug IDs. Dong et al. [14] analyzed a large number of inconsistencies by extracting software names and software versions in public security vulnerability reports, but they did not provide a practical solution to justify the incorrect software versions of the NVD. Anwar et al. [15] focused on the inconsistencies between the entries of the NVD and the CVE, and they explored methods for rectifying the vulnerability’s publication date, CWE class, CVSS rating, and the affected CPE information of the NVD, but we aim to use develop log to quantify the inaccuracy of the Know Affected Software Versions of the NVD.
Vulnerability Detection. Security patches are released to dis-

cover and ﬁx the vulnerabilities that have been released. This is also the most critical process for managing security systems. Using patches to discover vulnerabilities in the system has always been the focus of research in the security ﬁeld [45] . A vulnerability detection system with a high false positive rate or a high false negative rate is unusable. Many researchers [20] , [25] , [32] , [43] have studied the use of security patches for source code vulnerability detection. However, only based on patches, the error rate is still high. Li proposed VulDeePecker [23] using RNN training on code snippets related to library/API function calls to detect improper calls. The dataset they used in their work tried to compare with VUDDY, but no false positives of VUDDY were found. Further, considering silent security patches, Xiao et al. [46] proposed MVP and they enriched the dataset with commits obtained from their industrial collaborator, but such datasets were not publicly accessible. The methods proposed in [47] , [48] use trees and graphs as the representation methods for detecting vulnerabilities, and they cannot capture the exact nuance of vulnerabilities, so than they are not suitable
for determining which vulnerable versions are really affected by CVEs.
Reproducing Vulnerabilities. Reproducing a vulnerability is

the most convincing evidence for a vulnerable version. Her- cules [49] is based on symbolic execution to generate input examples to crash the software at the speciﬁed location. Sem- fuzz [50] makes use of CVE reports and Linux git logs to generatePoCexploits.Manyresearchproposesthetechniquesof generating patch that ﬁxes the vulnerability based on examples, while Huang et. al. [51] propose a property-based method to generate vulnerability patches, but their method is dependent on experts to specify the properties. As claimed in [17] reproduc- ing a vulnerability requires information such as conﬁgurations, trigger method, PoC, as well as a lot of manual work of experts, meanwhile these information are usually incomplete or unavail- able. It is very challenging especial for our task with a large sale of software versions.
Fuzzing combines symbolic execution and constraint solving

to detect vulnerabilities. Cassez et al. [21] use a static analysis tool for intermediate representation based on LLVM to check whether there is a run of a speciﬁed error block and reach the intermediate representation, which is mainly used to detect three types of vulnerabilities: zero error, pointer overﬂow, and dead code. In general, it is hard to perform directed fuzzing on Linux kernel. On the one hand, entering the kernel layer depends on the call of the application layer, which is equivalent to writing an application layer call for each CVE. On the other hand, Linux kernel is a large-scale system, which means that it has a very large number of conditional branch instructions. It is difﬁcult for fuzzing to ﬁnd the code where the diff hunk is located. Therefore, this type of methods are very expensive to employ in the context of vulnerability detection.
Our method has several assumptions which may limit its ap-

plication. First, developer logs and patches may be unavailable. Second, the software packages must be open-sourced. Third, although we propose log tracing to reduce the search space, the partial matching method still requires human experts to review some commits to determine whether the code is vulnerable, but the cost of this process is quite minor. In order to overcome these limitations, future work is needed. For the problem of unavailable patches, an approach is to identify and generate the patch from the respective software versions.
